4 min read
3 hours ago

--

Modern deployment usually starts with Docker containers, CI/CD pipelines, and cloud platforms. Those tools are genuinely useful — but they also hide the fundamentals underneath them.
Before any application can be deployed reliably, something more basic has to exist: a secure machine capable of serving it. That machine needs users, permissions, networking, a firewall, a web server, and encryption. None of these pieces are complicated in isolation, but together they form the foundation almost every production deployment quietly depends on.
Building that foundation by hand, at least once, changes how you think about infrastructure for good.
Starting With an Empty Linux Server
A freshly provisioned Linux server is deliberately minimal. No web server. No firewall rules. No application running. Almost no assumptions about how it will be used.
That blank slate forces a useful question:
What does a server actually need before it’s safe to expose to the internet?
The answer is surprisingly short — but every item on it matters.
Why Root Shouldn’t Be Your Daily User
The first change on any new server should be creating a regular administrative user.
Linux gives the root account unrestricted control over the entire operating system. That level of access is sometimes necessary, but using it for everyday work increases the blast radius of any mistake — a stray command, a misconfigured script, a compromised session.
Instead, Linux is built around a model where routine tasks run with limited permissions, and privileges are elevated temporarily, only when required. This is the principle of least privilege, and it shows up everywhere in operating system security because it limits how much damage a single mistake or compromised account can do.
Passwords Are Convenient. Keys Are Better.
Next: replace password-based SSH login with key-based authentication.
Passwords are vulnerable to guessing, reuse across sites, and brute-force attacks — and internet-facing servers get probed for weak passwords constantly.
SSH keys work differently. A private key stays on your local machine; the server only ever stores the matching public key. During login, the server verifies you hold the private key without that key ever being transmitted.
The result is a login method that’s both more secure and, once set up, more convenient — no password to type or remember. Disabling password authentication entirely also shuts down a whole category of automated attacks that never stop knocking on port 22.
A Firewall Is Not an Optional Feature
Every open port is a potential entry point. A firewall narrows that down to only what actually needs to be reachable.
For a basic web server, that’s typically just:

• SSH — for administration

• HTTP — for incoming web traffic

• HTTPS — for encrypted traffic
Everything else stays closed to the outside world. Fewer exposed services means fewer places for something to go wrong.
Serving Content With Nginx
With the operating system secured, the next job belongs to the web server.
Nginx has one core responsibility: receive incoming HTTP requests and decide how to handle them. For a static site, the flow is simple:

• A request arrives.

• Nginx maps the requested URL to a file on disk.

• That file is returned to the client.
No application runs. No database is queried. The server just reads a file and sends it over the network. That simplicity — and the performance it buys — is a big part of why Nginx is so widely used.
Understanding What HTTPS Actually Solves
For a lot of developers, HTTPS is just something that appears after running Certbot. It’s easy to stop there and never think about what actually changed.
What happened is more interesting than it looks. The server generated a cryptographic identity — a certificate and a private key. When a browser connects, it validates that identity against trusted certificate authorities before an encrypted session is established. From that point on, every request and response is protected in transit.
Without HTTPS, anyone positioned between client and server can potentially read or alter traffic. With it, intercepted traffic is unreadable without the negotiated session keys. That small padlock icon represents a genuinely large amount of cryptographic work happening automatically, in milliseconds.
Redirecting Every Request
Once HTTPS is available, plain HTTP has little reason to exist. Redirecting every HTTP request to HTTPS makes sure visitors always land on the encrypted version of the site.
Using a permanent (301) redirect also tells browsers and search engines that the secure URL is the canonical one. It’s a small configuration change with outsized security and SEO benefits.
Looking Beyond Commands
Server configuration is often taught as a list of terminal commands to memorize. That approach misses the point.
Every configuration choice exists because it solves a specific problem:

• Administrative users reduce unnecessary privilege.

• SSH keys strengthen authentication.

• Firewalls reduce exposure.

• Nginx serves requests efficiently.

• TLS protects data in transit.
Understanding why those pieces exist is more valuable than memorizing the commands that set them up. The commands can always be looked up again. Knowing why they matter is what turns “running commands” into engineering.

Try It Yourself: A Practical Guide for Beginners
Reading about this is fine, but the concepts click once you’ve actually done it. The walkthrough below takes about 20–30 minutes and works on any fresh Ubuntu server — a $4–6/month VPS from a provider like DigitalOcean, Linode, or Hetzner is enough, and most offer free trial credit.
I’ve also written this up as a full step-by-step task doc with extra detail and troubleshooting notes: Nginx Server Task — full guide.
If any of this sparked ideas, questions, or you’d just like to connect — I’m on LinkedIn: Connect with me on LinkedIn. Always open to talking infrastructure, sharing ideas, or hearing about opportunities.
